answerSummary: Canada's rental market is competitive, especially in Toronto and Vancouver. Average rents for a one-bedroom apartment in major cities range from $1,700 to $2,800/month. As a newcomer with no Canadian credit history, you will need to present alternative proof of your reliability — typically a larger deposit, employment letters, and international reference letters. Smaller cities, outer suburbs, and non-Toronto/Vancouver markets offer significantly better affordability.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: The Canadian Rental Market: A Realistic Picture

Canada's housing affordability challenge is well-documented. The Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) reports a national rental vacancy rate that has been below 2% in most major markets — meaning available units are quickly claimed. This creates a competitive market where prospective tenants often compete for the same listing.
Average monthly rents, one-bedroom apartment (2025 estimates):
— Canada's largest dedicated rental listing site
— Very widely used for private landlord listings (more risk of scams — see below)
— Aggregates listings across platforms with a map view
— Declining use but still present in some cities
— Organizations like COSTI (Toronto), MOSAIC (Vancouver), or local Immigrant Services Societies often maintain housing resources and referrals for newcomers
— Many cities have newcomer community Facebook groups where housing leads are shared
— Often have informal housing networks for members of specific communities
In a competitive market, being prepared with documentation gives you a significant advantage.
— Employment letter on company letterhead stating your job title, start date, and annual salary
— Last 1–2 pay stubs (if you have started work)
— Previous landlord references (international references are accepted, provide contact info)
For Newcomers Without Canadian Credit History
Without a Canadian credit score, landlords have less data to assess your reliability. You can compensate by offering:
First and last month's rent upfront
(standard in Ontario; ask in other provinces)
— A bank statement showing you have 3–6 months of rent in savings
Reference letter from a previous landlord
in your home country (with contact details for verification)
— If you have a Canadian friend, colleague, or family member willing to co-sign
Canada's rental laws are provincial. Key protections that exist in most provinces:

Landlords can only raise rent once per year, by a maximum percentage set by the provincial government (the "rent increase guideline" — 2.5% for 2025). Exempt: units first occupied after November 2018.
Annual increases limited to the CPI (inflation rate) — capped at 3% for 2025.
No province-wide rent control. Landlords can increase rent once every 365 days with 3 months' written notice.
Increases must be "reasonable"; tenants can contest increases at the Tribunal administratif du logement (TAL).

Landlords cannot evict you without cause. Valid reasons include non-payment of rent, damage to the property, or the landlord requiring the unit for personal use. The process requires formal written notice and, in most provinces, access to a tribunal before eviction can proceed.
Landlords are legally required to keep rental units in good repair. Tenants have the right to a unit that:
Is structurally sound with working heat, plumbing, and electricity
Meets local health and safety standards
Is maintained in a state of good repair
If a landlord fails to make necessary repairs, tenants can file a complaint with the provincial landlord-tenant tribunal.
Lease Agreements: What to Review Before Signing
Fixed-term (e.g., 1 year — you commit to that period) vs. month-to-month (more flexible, often more expensive)
Hydro (electricity), heat, water, parking, and laundry are sometimes included, sometimes not
to end the tenancy (typically 60 days in most provinces)
Whether you can sublet if you need to leave early
Ontario, British Columbia, and several other provinces have

that landlords must use. These protect tenants by ensuring key terms are disclosed. If a landlord refuses to use the standard form, tenants in Ontario have the right to withhold the last month's rent until provided with it.
Rental Scams: Red Flags for Newcomers
Newcomers are disproportionately targeted by rental fraud. Common scams:
— A beautiful unit listed significantly below market rent
and wants e-transfer or Western Union deposit before you view the unit
— Scammers often use non-traceable phone numbers
Requests to pay via cryptocurrency, gift cards, or wire transfer

Never pay any deposit before viewing a unit in person (or via a live video call if you are in another city) and confirming the landlord has the legal right to rent it (ask for proof of ownership or authorization from the owner).

Canada has a limited supply of non-market (subsidized) housing. Waitlists for social housing are typically very long (2–10+ years in major cities) and are generally not a viable option for recent newcomers. However, if you have a very low income, registering on the waitlist early makes sense.
Some provinces offer rent supplement programs:
Ontario Works (social assistance) includes a shelter allowance
Rental Assistance Program (RAP) for low-income families
A top-up to provincial/territorial housing assistance — combined with provincial programs for eligible low-income renters
Contact your provincial Ministry of Housing or local settlement agency for current availability and eligibility.

exampleScenarios.body: Nadia and her husband arrived from Morocco in February with two children. They needed a 3-bedroom apartment in Ottawa. Their budget was $2,200/month. They registered with the local settlement agency, which connected them with a housing navigator. Within 10 days, they had viewed 4 units and signed a lease on a 3-bedroom townhouse at $2,100/month in the suburbs. Because they had no Canadian credit history, they provided: a letter from Nadia's employer, proof of $15,000 in savings, and a reference from their previous Casablanca landlord (translated). The landlord accepted first and last month's rent and approved the application.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: In Canada, landlords cannot discriminate based on national or ethnic origin, place of origin, or citizenship — these are protected grounds under the Canadian Human Rights Act and provincial human rights codes. However, landlords can decline applicants based on insufficient income or lack of credit history, which disproportionately affects newcomers in practice.
faqItems.question: Can a landlord refuse to rent to me because I am a newcomer?

faqItems.answer: Landlords often run a credit check to confirm you have a history of paying obligations. As a newcomer with no Canadian credit, the check may return "no file found" rather than a bad score. Supplement this by providing income proof and references proactively.
faqItems.question: What is a "credit check" in the rental context?

faqItems.answer: Rules vary by province. In Ontario, landlords are limited to collecting first and last month's rent — no separate damage deposit is permitted. In BC and Alberta, damage deposits of up to half a month's rent are standard. Always get a receipt. *Sources: CMHC Rental Market Report 2025 (cmhc-schl.gc.ca); provincial Residential Tenancies Acts (Ontario, BC, Alberta, Quebec). This article is for educational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice.*
faqItems.question: How much should I budget for a damage deposit?

answerSummary: Your Social Insurance Number (SIN) is a 9-digit number issued by the federal government. You need it to work legally in Canada, file taxes, and access government benefits. Most newcomers can apply at a Service Canada office the same week they arrive — bring your immigration documents and expect a 15–30 minute visit.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: What Is a SIN and Why Do You Need It?

A Social Insurance Number (SIN) is Canada's way of tracking your employment income and tax contributions. Think of it like a Social Security Number in the United States or a National Insurance Number in the United Kingdom.
You will need your SIN to:
Start a job legally in Canada
File your income tax return with the CRA
Open certain bank accounts and investment accounts (RRSP, TFSA, FHSA)
Apply for government benefits like the Canada Child Benefit (CCB) and Employment Insurance (EI)
You should apply for your SIN as soon as possible after arriving.
Without it, your employer cannot process your first paycheque properly, and you may lose out on tax refunds.
Who Is Eligible to Get a SIN?
You can apply for a SIN if you are:
A temporary resident with a valid work permit
A refugee claimant with authorization to work
If you are here on a study permit and do not have a work permit, you may still get a SIN if your permit includes a condition that allows you to work on- or off-campus.
If your immigration status is temporary (e.g., work permit), your SIN will begin with a
and will have an expiry date linked to your permit. You must renew it when your permit is renewed.
proving your identity and immigration status:
If your primary document does not show your current address in Canada, bring a secondary document such as a utility bill, lease agreement, or bank statement.
How to Apply for a SIN
Option 1: Apply in Person at Service Canada (Recommended)
This is the fastest option. You will receive your SIN the same day in most cases.

Find your nearest Service Canada Centre at [canada.ca/en/employment-social-development/corporate/portfolio/service-canada.html](https://www.canada.ca/en/employment-social-development/corporate/portfolio/service-canada.html)
Tell the agent you are applying for a SIN
They will issue your SIN verbally or on a confirmation letter —
there is no physical SIN card anymore
Wait times vary. Arriving when the office opens (typically 8:30 AM) reduces waiting.
Service Canada has an online application portal for eligible newcomers.
Upload clear photos of your documents
The online process is convenient but slower. If you need to start work quickly, in-person is better.
Mail applications are available but take up to
and carry a risk of your original documents being lost in transit. This option is not recommended unless you are in a remote area with no nearby Service Canada office.
Your SIN is sensitive personal information. Misuse can lead to identity theft and tax fraud.
Do not carry your SIN confirmation letter
Store it in a secure place at home (a locked drawer or safe)
Only share your SIN when legally required (your employer, bank, or the CRA)
Merchants, landlords, and most businesses do
have a legal right to ask for your SIN
If you believe your SIN has been misused, contact Service Canada at 1-800-206-7218.
What If You Lose Your SIN Number?
There is no longer a physical SIN card. Your SIN is on file with the government. If you have forgotten your number, you can find it on:
Your T4 slip from a previous employer
Your Notice of Assessment from the CRA
To get an official confirmation letter, visit a Service Canada office in person with your ID.

exampleScenarios.body: Maria arrived in Toronto from Mexico on a Permanent Resident visa in January. On her third day in Canada, she took the subway to the nearest Service Canada office with her COPR letter and passport. She arrived at 9:00 AM, waited about 20 minutes, and left with her SIN number written on an official letter. That afternoon, she gave her new employer the number so her payroll could be set up correctly.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: No. Applying for a SIN is completely free. Be cautious of third-party websites that charge a fee to help you apply — the process is straightforward and free at Service Canada.
faqItems.question: Does getting a SIN cost anything?

faqItems.answer: You can start a job, but your employer is required by law to collect your SIN within 3 days of you starting work. You should apply before your first day if at all possible.
faqItems.question: Can I work without a SIN?

faqItems.answer: A SIN beginning with 9 is issued to temporary residents (work permit holders, etc.). It has an expiry date and must be renewed when your immigration status changes or your permit expires. When you become a permanent resident, apply for a new SIN that does not start with 9.
faqItems.question: My SIN starts with a 9. What does that mean?

faqItems.answer: No. You are only ever issued one SIN in your lifetime. If you are a permanent resident and previously had a temporary SIN (starting with 9), Service Canada will issue you a new number and cancel the old one. *This article is for educational purposes only. For the most current information, visit the official Service Canada website at canada.ca.*
faqItems.question: Can I have more than one SIN?

answerSummary: Canada has an extensive social safety net with dozens of benefit programs. Many are available to permanent residents from the moment they arrive; temporary residents have access to some benefits after a qualifying period. Most benefits require you to file an annual income tax return to receive them — this is the single most important action to unlock ongoing government support.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: The Key to Unlocking Benefits: File Your Taxes
Before going through each benefit, there is one rule that applies to almost all of them:
File your income tax return every year, even if you have no income.

The CRA uses your filed return to determine eligibility for the vast majority of benefit programs. Many newcomers miss out on hundreds or thousands of dollars in annual payments simply because they did not realize they needed to file.
A quarterly, tax-free payment to help low- and middle-income Canadians offset the Goods and Services Tax (GST) and Harmonized Sales Tax (HST) they pay on purchases.
Canadian residents with low-to-moderate income. Includes permanent residents. Temporary residents may qualify.
File your tax return. The CRA automatically assesses eligibility.
Payments are made quarterly (January, April, July, October).
Monthly, tax-free payments for families raising children under 18.
Canadian residents (including PR holders). Temporary residents qualify after 18 months.
Apply through CRA My Account, at birth registration (for newborns), or via Form RC66.
See our full guide: [Canada Child Benefit Guide](/articles/canada-child-benefit-ccb-guide)
A refundable tax credit for low-income working Canadians and permanent residents. As of 2023, advance payments are sent automatically quarterly.
Working Canadians/PRs with low income. Must be 19+ or have a spouse/dependant. Single adults: net income under ~$35,000; families: net income under ~$60,000 (thresholds vary by province).
Claim Schedule 6 on your tax return. Tax software handles this automatically.
A new federal program (rolled out 2024–2025) providing dental coverage for Canadians without existing dental insurance and with a family income under $90,000.
Enrolled in phases — as of 2025, open to adults 18+ and families meeting the income threshold. Permanent residents and some temporary residents are eligible.
Basic dental (exams, cleanings, X-rays, fillings, extractions). The plan covers 60–100% of costs based on income.
Apply through Service Canada online or by phone at 1-833-537-4342.

A quarterly payment to eligible Canadians in provinces covered by the federal carbon pricing backstop (currently Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, PEI, and Newfoundland).
Canadian residents in covered provinces. Permanent residents are eligible.
Payment amounts vary by province and family composition.
File your tax return. Eligibility is automatic.
A new federal benefit (fully launched 2025) providing up to $2,400/year to low-income working-age Canadians with disabilities.
Ages 18–64, eligible for the Disability Tax Credit (DTC), with income below the threshold.
Apply for the Disability Tax Credit first (Form T2201, certified by a medical professional), then apply for the CDB through Service Canada.
Beyond regular job-loss EI, special EI benefits include:
15 weeks at 55% of earnings for birth parents
Up to 26 weeks at 55% if unable to work due to illness
For those caring for a critically ill family member
8. Old Age Security (OAS) and Guaranteed Income Supplement (GIS)
These are retirement-age benefits, but newcomers should understand them for long-term planning:

A monthly pension for Canadians 65+ who have lived in Canada for at least 10 years as an adult (40 years for the maximum benefit). Newcomers who arrive as adults will receive a partial OAS based on years of Canadian residency.
Additional monthly payment for low-income OAS recipients.
As a newcomer, you also have access to
funded by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC):
Free English and French language classes for permanent residents
Résumé help, job search assistance, credential recognition
Find settlement services near you at the IRCC settlement portal: ircc.canada.ca/english/newcomers/services
Each province adds its own programs on top of federal benefits:
Provincial benefits are typically assessed automatically through the same tax return process as federal benefits.

exampleScenarios.body:
The Okafor family — two adults and two young children — arrived in Ontario in January as permanent residents. By April (after filing their return), they were receiving:
- CCB: ~$1,050/month for two children
- GST/HST Credit: ~$700/year (quarterly)
- Ontario Child Benefit: ~$150/month
- Canada Workers Benefit advance: ~$200/month (lower-income spouse)
Total annual benefit income from government programs: approximately $16,200 — significant support during a challenging period of settlement, all unlocked simply by filing their first tax return.

answerSummary: Canada's healthcare system is publicly funded and administered by each province. Most services (doctor visits, hospital stays, diagnostic tests) are free once you have your provincial health card — but most provinces impose a waiting period of up to 3 months before coverage starts. During this window, you must have private insurance. Long-term, provincial coverage still leaves significant gaps (dental, vision, prescription drugs) that employer benefits or private insurance should fill.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Canada operates under the Canada Health Act (1984), which establishes principles that provinces must follow to receive federal funding: universality, comprehensiveness, accessibility, portability, and public administration.
Every Canadian resident is entitled to medically necessary hospital and physician services without direct charges
The system is funded through taxes, not premiums (with a few exceptions — see Quebec below)
(not an insurance card) that you present at any doctor's office or hospital
Most provinces impose a waiting period before your provincial health coverage activates. This is one of the most urgent financial risks newcomers face.
are the most newcomer-friendly for immediate coverage — a meaningful advantage for those choosing where to settle.
Applying for Your Provincial Health Card
Apply immediately upon arriving — even in provinces with a waiting period, the clock starts from your registration date, not your application date.
Apply at any ServiceOntario location. Bring:
Valid photo ID (passport, PR card, driver's licence)
Proof of Ontario residency (lease agreement, utility bill, bank statement)
Processing time: Your interim card is issued the same day. Your photo health card arrives by mail within 4–6 weeks.
Apply online at hibc.gov.bc.ca or at a Service BC location. BC's MSP has been premium-free since January 2020.
Apply at an Alberta registry agent or online. Coverage begins the day you establish Alberta residency.
Apply at a RAMQ service point. French and English service available. Bring your immigration documents.
Family doctor and specialist visits (no co-pay)
Diagnostic tests ordered by a doctor (blood tests, X-rays, ultrasounds, MRIs)
Mental health visits with a physician (GP or psychiatrist)
Prescription drugs (except in specific provincial programs for seniors, social assistance recipients, and some low-income residents — see below)
Private or semi-private hospital rooms (provincial plans cover ward beds only)
Services performed outside Canada (travel medical coverage)
Prescription Drug Coverage: The Gap Newcomers Miss
Prescription drugs are not covered by provincial health plans for most working-age adults. This surprises many newcomers from countries with universal drug coverage.
Your options for prescription drug coverage:
Most full-time jobs include drug coverage (typically 80–100% of eligible drug costs). Confirm when your employer coverage starts — there is often a 1–3 month waiting period.
Most provinces offer drug coverage for low-income residents, social assistance recipients, or seniors:
Ontario Drug Benefit (ODB) — for social assistance recipients, seniors, and some low-income residents
Fair PharmaCare — income-tested sliding scale coverage for all BC residents
Alberta Blue Cross coverage for those on AISH or Income Support
Mandatory prescription drug insurance — all Quebec residents must have drug coverage either through an employer plan or the provincial RAMQ drug plan (premiums apply)

Individual health insurance plans covering drugs, dental, and vision. Cost varies widely — approximately $80–$180/month for a single adult basic plan. Most useful during the employer waiting period or for self-employed newcomers.
Providers include: Manulife, Sun Life, Blue Cross, GreenShield Canada, Industrial Alliance.
As of 2024–2025, the federal government launched the Canadian Dental Care Plan — a major expansion of dental coverage for Canadians without existing dental insurance.
No existing dental benefits (through employer, provincial, or private plan)
Canadian resident with a valid SIN
Permanent residents are eligible; temporary residents may qualify based on status
Does not cover orthodontics or most cosmetic procedures
Online at canada.ca/dental or by calling 1-833-537-4342.
Bridging the Waiting Period: Private Interim Insurance
If your province has a 3-month waiting period,
you must arrange private coverage for this period.
A serious medical event — hospitalization, emergency surgery, a complicated childbirth — can cost tens of thousands of dollars without coverage.
If you purchased travel insurance before arriving in Canada, extending it through the waiting period is often cost-effective.
Several insurers sell policies designed specifically for the provincial waiting period:
Manulife — CoverMe Visitors to Canada / Newcomer Plan
Costs typically range from $100–$250/month depending on your age, health history, and coverage level.
What to look for in an interim policy:
Coverage for emergency hospitalization and surgery
No exclusion for pre-existing conditions (or confirm what is excluded)
Coverage amount of at least $1 million (CAD)
Under the Canada Health Act, your provincial health coverage generally follows you when you travel within Canada for temporary stays. This is called

Practically: if you are visiting BC while covered by OHIP (Ontario), you can receive emergency care and the cost is billed between provinces. Non-emergency care may not be covered if you are in another province temporarily.
— Quebec has limited reciprocal billing arrangements with other provinces. If you are a Quebec resident travelling elsewhere in Canada (or vice versa), you may face some out-of-pocket costs.
Long-Term: What You Will Still Need to Arrange Privately
Even after your provincial health card is active, significant gaps remain. Most Canadians address these through:
— The most common source of dental, drug, and paramedical coverage (see our guide: [Understanding Your Canadian Employer Benefits](/articles/understanding-canadian-employer-benefits))
— For self-employed newcomers or those without employer benefits
— Canadian Dental Care Plan, provincial drug programs (income-tested)

exampleScenarios.body: Tariq and his family arrived in Ontario in June. Knowing they faced a 3-month OHIP waiting period, Tariq purchased a newcomer health insurance plan from Blue Cross for $340/month (covering himself, his wife, and two children). In July, his youngest daughter had a fever and ear infection — the plan covered the walk-in clinic visit and $80 in antibiotics. In September, OHIP activated. Tariq then joined his employer's group health plan (starting in October per the 30-day waiting period) for extended coverage. He subsequently enrolled the family in the Canadian Dental Care Plan as his employer plan did not include dental for dependants in the first year.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: In most provinces, yes — you typically need a referral from a family doctor (GP) to see a specialist, and the wait for specialists can be long. Walk-in clinics can provide some immediate care and referrals while you are waiting to be assigned a family doctor.
faqItems.question: Do I need a referral to see a specialist in Canada?

faqItems.answer: Visits to a psychiatrist (a medical doctor) are covered by provincial health plans with a doctor's referral. Visits to a psychologist, therapist, or counsellor are generally **not covered** provincially — these typically fall under employer extended health benefits (paramedical services). Some provincial programs offer subsidized mental health counselling; check your province.
faqItems.question: Is mental health care covered?

faqItems.answer: OHIP (or any provincial health plan) covers medically necessary physician and hospital services at no cost. Private insurance covers the gaps OHIP does not — prescription drugs, dental, vision, physiotherapy, and so on. You need both for comprehensive coverage.
faqItems.question: What is the difference between OHIP and a private insurance plan?

faqItems.answer: Yes — generally significantly cheaper, due to federal regulation. However, without drug insurance, costs can still be substantial for specialty medications. *Sources: Canada Health Act R.S.C. 1985, c. C-6; Health Canada (canada.ca/health-care); OHIP (ontario.ca/ohip); BC MSP (hibc.gov.bc.ca); AHCIP (alberta.ca/ahcip); CDCP (canada.ca/dental). This article is for educational purposes only.*

answerSummary: Health insurance for international students in Canada varies by province and institution. In most provinces, international students on a study permit are **not covered by the provincial health plan** and must purchase private health insurance. Ontario is the notable exception — international students at most Ontario universities are enrolled in UHIP (University Health Insurance Plan), which provides provincial-equivalent coverage. British Columbia and Alberta cover international students under their provincial plans.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Provincial Health Coverage for International Students: Province by Province
This is the most important thing to know — your health coverage depends entirely on which province your institution is in.
UHIP is a provincially mandated health insurance program for international students at Ontario universities and their dependants.
Sun Life Financial on behalf of the Council of Ontario Universities
International students at participating Ontario universities and their eligible dependants (spouse, dependent children) who are in Canada with you.
Approximately $650–$750 per student; additional for dependants.
Prescription drugs (limited coverage — check the formulary)
Emergency care within Canada and limited out-of-province coverage
Preventive care that is not medically necessary

If you have equivalent private coverage (through a parent's plan or your own private policy), you may be able to opt out of UHIP during your institution's opt-out window (typically the first 30 days of each school year). You must demonstrate equivalent or superior coverage to do so.
British Columbia: MSP Coverage for International Students
International students in BC on a valid study permit are eligible for the provincial health plan (MSP — Medical Services Plan) with

Apply online at hibc.gov.bc.ca or in person at a Service BC location. Bring your study permit and passport. Coverage begins on the date you apply (or your arrival date if you apply within 3 months of arriving).
Doctor visits, hospital care, diagnostic services — the same as for permanent residents. MSP does not cover prescription drugs, dental, or vision.

Most BC post-secondary institutions (UBC, SFU, BCIT, etc.) include a mandatory student health and dental plan in their student fees (~$300–$600/year). This covers prescription drugs, dental, and some paramedical services on top of MSP.
Alberta: AHCIP Coverage for International Students
International students in Alberta on a valid study permit are eligible for Alberta's provincial health plan (AHCIP) immediately upon establishing Alberta residency.
Apply at an Alberta registry agent or online. Coverage starts on the first day of the month after your application date (so apply immediately on arrival).
Like BC, Alberta universities typically include a supplemental health and dental plan in student fees.
Quebec and Other Provinces: Private Insurance Required
(the provincial health plan). You must purchase private health insurance that meets Quebec's requirements.
The Certificate d'acceptation du Québec (CAQ) for study requires proof of health insurance. See our dedicated guide: [Studying in Quebec: CAQ Requirements](/articles/studying-in-quebec-caq).

Most Quebec universities (McGill, Concordia, UdeM, Université Laval) automatically enroll international students in a mandatory health and dental insurance plan (~$700–$900/year) that meets provincial requirements.
Across Canada, most universities and colleges include a supplemental health and dental plan in student fees —
even in provinces where the provincial plan covers you
. This is separate from the provincial plan and covers the gaps it leaves.
Prescription drugs: 60–80% up to an annual maximum
Dental (basic): 60–80% coverage for cleanings, fillings, X-rays
Paramedical: Physiotherapy, massage, psychologist ($200–$500 per practitioner)
Travel insurance: Emergency coverage outside your province
Typically included in student fees — approximately $300–$700/year depending on institution. Check your institution's student union for details.

If you have equivalent coverage elsewhere, most institutions allow opt-out during a defined window. Most international students should keep the student plan as it is well-priced for the coverage offered.
What International Students Typically Need to Budget for Healthcare

exampleScenarios.body: Yuki from Japan enrolled at the University of Toronto. She was automatically enrolled in UHIP at $720 for the year and in the student union dental plan at $340. In November, she needed antibiotics for a respiratory infection — the walk-in clinic visit was covered by UHIP at no charge, and her antibiotics cost $24 (partially covered by the UHIP drug benefit). In January, she needed new glasses — the student dental/vision plan reimbursed $150 of her $280 purchase. Her total out-of-pocket healthcare for the year was under $500.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Coverage at colleges varies more than at universities. Check with your institution's student services office to confirm what health coverage is included in your student fees and whether provincial coverage applies. If no plan is provided, arrange private insurance before arrival.
faqItems.question: I'm studying at a small college, not a university. Do I still have coverage?

faqItems.answer: Yes. University health plans (UHIP, student union plans) are annual and renew each September/October with your student registration. Keep your enrollment current.
faqItems.question: My study permit is for 2 years. Do I need to renew my insurance each year?

faqItems.answer: Emergency care at a hospital is never withheld in Canada regardless of insurance status — you will receive care. However, you may receive a bill for services if you are not insured. Enroll in your coverage immediately upon arrival to avoid this.
faqItems.question: What if I have a medical emergency and am not yet enrolled in a plan?

faqItems.answer: Canada has reciprocal healthcare agreements with some countries (notably some provinces have bilateral arrangements). However, these generally apply to temporary visitors, not long-term residents on study permits. Do not assume your home country coverage is sufficient — verify directly with the consulate. *Sources: Ontario UHIP (uhip.ca); BC MSP (hibc.gov.bc.ca); Alberta AHCIP (alberta.ca/ahcip); Health Canada. This article is for educational purposes only.*

answerSummary: Canada uses a graduated (progressive) income tax system. You pay a lower rate on your first dollars of income and higher rates only on income above each threshold — not on all your income at once. In addition to federal tax, each province charges its own income tax on top. Your total tax bill combines both layers, but the average effective rate most Canadians actually pay is much lower than their marginal (top bracket) rate.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Canada's Progressive Tax System: The Key Concept
The single most important thing to understand about Canadian income tax is this:
moving into a higher bracket only affects the portion of your income above that threshold.
It does not cause all your income to be taxed at the higher rate.

If you earn $60,000 in Ontario in 2025, you do not pay 26.5% (the combined bracket for $57,375–$100,392) on all $60,000. You pay 15% on the first $57,375 federally, and 26.5% only on the last $2,625 that sits above the threshold.
This is one of the most common misconceptions newcomers (and many long-time Canadians) have about the tax system.
Canada's federal government taxes income at graduated rates. The 2025 federal brackets are:
of $16,129 (federal, 2025) is deducted from your income. This means the first $16,129 of income is not taxed at all federally.
Provincial Income Tax: A Second Layer
Each province and territory charges its own income tax on top of the federal tax. Provincial rates and brackets vary significantly.
2025 Combined Marginal Rates at $80,000 Income (Federal + Provincial)
Quebec administers its own separate income tax system through Revenu Québec, not the CRA, which is why Quebec residents file two tax returns each year (one federal, one provincial).
Marginal Rate vs. Effective Rate: What You Actually Pay
The rate that applies to your next dollar of income (top bracket you are in).
Your total tax as a percentage of your total income.
For most Canadians, the effective rate is significantly lower than the marginal rate because lower brackets apply to the first portions of income.

— Each dollar contributed reduces taxable income by one dollar
Union dues and professional membership fees
(if moving for work or school, 40 km closer rule)

How Tax Is Collected: The Withholding System
In Canada, your employer deducts estimated income tax from each paycheque and remits it directly to the CRA. This is called
The amount withheld is based on your TD1 form — a form you fill out when you start a new job that tells your employer about your expected income and basic credits (such as the Basic Personal Amount).
When you file your annual tax return, the CRA reconciles:
Tax actually owed based on your full income picture
If more was withheld than you owe →

The TD1 Form: Setting Up Withholding Correctly
is a form you complete for your employer when you start a job. It tells your employer how much tax to deduct from each paycheque.

The default TD1 applies the Basic Personal Amount only. If you have additional credits (e.g., eligible tuition, disability amounts), claim them on the form to reduce withholding and improve your monthly cash flow.
CPP, EI, and Other Payroll Deductions
Before income tax, three other deductions appear on every paycheque. These are not income tax but are often confused with it:
A retirement contribution. Employee rate: 5.95% on earnings between $3,500 and $73,200 (2025). See our full guide: [CPP and EI Deductions Explained](/articles/cpp-ei-deductions-explained-newcomers).
An insurance premium. Employee rate: 1.64% on insurable earnings up to $65,700 (2025).
The graduated federal + provincial tax described in this article.
If you arrived in Canada partway through the year, you file as a
You report only Canadian income from the date you became a resident
Your Basic Personal Amount and most credits are prorated based on the number of days you were a resident
You may also need to report world income for the period before arrival if Canada has a tax treaty with your home country
explains part-year filing in detail. Tax software handles the proration automatically when you enter your arrival date.

exampleScenarios.body: Fatima arrived in Canada in July 2025 and worked for 6 months, earning $38,000. She completed a TD1 and TD1ON for her Ontario employer, claiming only the Basic Personal Amount. Her employer withheld approximately $5,100 in income tax. When she filed her return in March 2026, entering her July arrival date, the software prorated her Basic Personal Amount to 6/12 of the full year. Her actual federal + Ontario tax came to approximately $4,400 — resulting in a $700 refund. She also qualified for a partial GST/HST credit automatically.

faqItems.answer: Withholding is based on estimates. If you had deductions (RRSP contributions, moving expenses) or credits not reflected in your TD1, your refund will be larger. You can file an updated TD1 at any time during the year to reduce over-withholding.

answerSummary: CRA My Account is the Canada Revenue Agency's secure online portal. Once registered, you can view all your tax slips, check RRSP and TFSA contribution room, track benefit payments (GST credit, CCB), and manage your direct deposit information. Registration requires your SIN and either a previous tax return or a confirmed bank account.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: CRA My Account is your personal online hub for all things tax and benefits in Canada. Think of it like an online banking portal, but for your relationship with the federal government.
View all your T4, T5, and other tax slips filed by employers and financial institutions
Check your RRSP contribution room and TFSA contribution room
See your Notice of Assessment for past years
Track benefit payments (GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit)
Set up or change your direct deposit for tax refunds and benefits
File a tax return (via NETFILE-certified software linked to your account)
Request a change to a previously filed return
Authorize a tax representative (e.g., accountant) to access your account
A previous year's Canadian tax return
(the CRA will ask you to confirm a number from it), OR
mailed to your address (takes 5–10 business days)
and you have not filed a return yet, you will need to wait for the mailed security code option or file your first return and then register afterward.
Enter your SIN, date of birth, and postal code
If you have a previous return: enter a line item from it (e.g., Line 15000 — total income)
If you do not: request a security code by mail
(or link to a Sign-In Partner — see below)
If you bank with one of Canada's major banks (RBC, TD, Scotiabank, BMO, CIBC, Desjardins, etc.), you can use your
to sign into CRA My Account via the "Sign-In Partner" option. This skips the security code step and provides immediate access.
What to Do First After Registering
Once logged in, your immediate priorities as a newcomer:
— Go to "Manage direct deposit" and enter your Canadian bank account info. This ensures any refunds or benefit payments go directly to your account rather than arriving by cheque.
— Under "Notification preferences," sign up for email notifications so the CRA emails you when new correspondence is available. This prevents important notices from being missed in paper mail.
Check your TFSA and RRSP room
— Under "RRSP and savings plans," you will see your available contribution room for each account.
— Under "Tax information slips," you can see all slips on file, useful for cross-referencing with what your employer or bank sent you.
"The information you entered does not match our records"

Double-check your SIN, date of birth, and current postal code. If you recently moved, the CRA may have your old address — use the postal code on file with the CRA (from your last return or immigration record).
Too many failed login attempts locks the account. Call CRA at 1-800-959-8281 to unlock.
Allow 10–15 business days. If it still has not arrived, call CRA to request a new one or try the Sign-In Partner option.

exampleScenarios.body: Omar arrived from Egypt in April and started work immediately. He applied for his SIN in his first week. In late September, he registered for CRA My Account using the Sign-In Partner option through his RBC online banking. Within minutes he was logged in. He set up direct deposit to his RBC chequing account, reviewed the single T4 on file (from a part-year job), and confirmed his TFSA contribution room for the year. When tax season arrived in March, his refund was deposited directly within 8 days of filing.

faqItems.answer: The CRA uses multi-factor authentication (MFA) and encryption. However, CRA accounts have been targeted by identity theft scams in the past. Always log in directly at canada.ca — never through links in emails, and never share your login credentials.

faqItems.answer: Yes. Under "Benefits and credits," you can see all upcoming and past payments for the GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, and other federal benefits. If you are owed a benefit that is not showing up, check whether your tax return has been filed and assessed.
faqItems.question: Can I use CRA My Account to check if I am owed money?

faqItems.answer: No. You can file without a My Account using NETFILE-certified software. But having My Account makes it easier to access your slips, track your return's status, and avoid errors. *This article is for educational purposes only. Always access CRA services through the official canada.ca website.*

For non-registered savings (beyond your registered limits), you simply open a regular investment account — often called a "taxable" or "non-registered" account.
For most newcomers, the TFSA's flexibility (no tax on withdrawals, ever) and simplicity (no room calculation based on prior income) makes it the right starting point.
Choosing Where to Open an Account (Brokers)
You pick your own investments. Lower fees but requires more engagement.

is an excellent choice to start.
What to Invest In: All-In-One ETFs
If you are opening a self-directed account, the simplest and most evidence-backed approach is a
. These are funds that hold thousands of companies across the world in one ticker symbol, and automatically rebalance.
Popular all-in-one ETFs on Canadian exchanges:
Management fees (MER) are approximately 0.20% per year — extremely low.

Open a TFSA at Questrade or Wealthsimple

The Investor's Mindset: What to Expect
In any given year, your investments may be down 10–30%. This is normal and temporary over a long time horizon.
Time in the market beats timing the market.
Investing consistently beats trying to guess the best moment.
A 1% fee difference over 30 years can cost tens of thousands of dollars. ETFs and robo-advisors keep fees low.
Do not check your balance daily.
Monthly or quarterly reviews are plenty.

answerSummary: The First Home Savings Account (FHSA) is a relatively new registered account (launched in 2023) that helps first-time homebuyers save for a down payment. Like an RRSP, contributions are tax-deductible. Like a TFSA, qualifying withdrawals (to buy your first home) are completely tax-free. You can contribute up to $8,000 per year and $40,000 lifetime.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: The FHSA is the most tax-advantaged account in Canada for one very specific purpose: saving for your first home purchase. It combines the best feature of two other accounts:
Contributions reduce your taxable income (i.e., you get a tax deduction and potentially a refund)
Qualifying withdrawals to buy a first home are completely tax-free
If you are planning to buy a home in Canada within the next 1–15 years, the FHSA should be a priority.
To open an FHSA, you must:
(or 19 in provinces where the age of majority is 19)
— meaning you have not owned a qualifying home that you lived in at any point during the current calendar year, or during the preceding four years
Most newcomers qualify as first-time homebuyers because they did not own a home in Canada in the preceding four years.
If you open an FHSA in 2025 but only contribute $3,000, you can contribute up to $13,000 in 2026 ($8,000 for 2026 + $5,000 carried forward from 2025).
Tax Benefits: Both Coming In and Going Out
On the Way In — Tax Deduction
Like an RRSP, every dollar you contribute to an FHSA is deducted from your taxable income. If you contribute $8,000 and your marginal tax rate is 40%, your tax refund is approximately $3,200.
Unlike an RRSP, you can also carry forward unused deductions — so you do not have to claim the deduction in the same year you contribute.
On the Way Out — Tax-Free Withdrawal
, all withdrawals from the FHSA are completely tax-free: contributions, growth, and all. You pay zero tax.
This is the key advantage over the RRSP's Home Buyers' Plan — RRSP withdrawals via the HBP must be repaid over 15 years.
FHSA withdrawals for a first home never need to be repaid.
What If You Don't Buy a Home?

If you open an FHSA but never end up buying a home (or if you close the account after 15 years), you can transfer the balance to your RRSP or RRIF with no tax consequences. The balance is not lost — it simply becomes RRSP savings.
FHSA vs RRSP Home Buyers' Plan (HBP): Which Is Better?
— maxing out the FHSA first, then also using the HBP from their RRSP for additional funds.
All major Canadian banks and most investment platforms now offer FHSAs:
Open the account the same way you would a TFSA or RRSP — in person or online, with your SIN.

exampleScenarios.body:
Michael arrived from the Philippines in 2024 as a permanent resident. He knew he wanted to buy a condo in 3–5 years. He opened an FHSA at Questrade and contributed the maximum $8,000. At his marginal rate of 32%, this gave him a tax refund of approximately $2,560 which he reinvested in his TFSA. Over 5 years, contributing $8,000/year and earning 6% annually, his FHSA would grow to approximately $47,600 — and every dollar of that would come out completely tax-free when he used it toward his down payment.
Use our [FHSA Calculator](/tools/rrsp-refund) to model your own scenario.

faqItems.answer: The home must be a qualifying home (generally: a residential property in Canada you intend to occupy as your principal residence within 1 year of buying). This includes condos, detached houses, townhomes, and duplexes.
faqItems.question: Can I use my FHSA for any home in Canada?

faqItems.answer: Yes. Each of you can have your own FHSA with your own $40,000 lifetime limit. Together, you could save up to $80,000 in FHSAs for a combined down payment.
faqItems.question: Can my spouse and I each have an FHSA?

faqItems.answer: Yes, as long as you are a Canadian resident, at least 18, and a first-time homebuyer. You do not need to have filed a tax return first (unlike the RRSP, which requires prior income).
faqItems.question: I just arrived in Canada. Can I open an FHSA immediately?

faqItems.answer: Over-contributions are subject to a 1% per month penalty on the excess amount until corrected. Always confirm your available room before contributing. *This article is for educational purposes only. FHSA rules may change — verify current details at canada.ca/fhsa.*

answerSummary: You can open a Canadian bank account within your first week of arriving, even before you have a job or credit history. All major banks offer special newcomer programs with reduced or waived fees for 1–2 years. You will need your passport, proof of immigration status, and a Canadian address.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Why Opening a Bank Account Comes First
Before you do almost anything else financially in Canada, you need a bank account. You will need it to:
Receive your paycheque via direct deposit
Pay rent (most landlords require electronic transfer or a cheque)
Set up bill payments for phone, internet, and utilities
Apply for a credit card to start building your Canadian credit score
Receive government payments like the Canada Child Benefit
Without a Canadian bank account, even basic daily life becomes difficult. The good news: Canadian banks actively compete for newcomer customers and make it easy to get started.
Most banks require two pieces of identification, with at least one being a government-issued photo ID:
Credit card or bank card from your home country
You will also need to provide a
— even a temporary address such as a friend's home, a hotel, or a short-term rental is usually accepted.
You do not need a job, credit history, or a minimum deposit to open a basic chequing account in Canada. Banks are required by law to open a basic account for eligible customers.
Types of Accounts You Will Need
Your everyday account for spending, bill payments, and receiving your salary. Most newcomer programs offer fee-free chequing for the first year.
A place to set aside money. High-interest savings accounts (HISAs) at online banks often offer better interest rates than the big banks.
Once you have been a Canadian resident for at least a year and have filed a tax return, you should open a TFSA for investing or saving — you do not pay tax on growth inside it.
For now, focus on getting a chequing account open first. You can add other account types over time.
Newcomer Banking Programs at Canada's Major Banks
All of Canada's major banks offer some form of newcomer banking program. Features change regularly — always confirm current offers directly with the bank.
(e.g., Simplii Financial, Tangerine, EQ Bank) often have no monthly fees permanently and competitive savings rates, but may offer less newcomer-specific support.
Choose a bank based on location, language support, or program features
Book an appointment online or walk in during business hours
Bring your documents (passport + immigration document + Canadian address)
Ask about the newcomer program specifically and confirm the fee waiver period
Leave with a bank card (debit card) typically within 5–7 business days
Most banks now allow online account opening, but some require in-person verification for newcomers without an existing Canadian credit profile. If the online process stalls, visit a branch.
Comparing Accounts: What to Look For
When choosing a newcomer banking package, look beyond just the free-fee period:
Monthly fee after the promo period ends
Number of free transactions per month
— useful while you are getting settled
— many major banks offer service in Mandarin, Cantonese, Punjabi, Arabic, and other languages

exampleScenarios.body: Ahmed arrived from Egypt in September on a work permit. He visited a TD Bank branch near his new apartment with his passport, his work permit, and a copy of his lease agreement (for the address). The appointment took about 45 minutes. He left with a new chequing account, a debit card on the way in the mail, and a secured credit card application approved — all under the TD New to Canada package at no fee for the first year.

faqItems.answer: Yes. A SIN is not required to open a basic chequing account. However, you will need a SIN to open investment accounts like a TFSA or RRSP, and banks may ask for it for tax reporting purposes.
faqItems.question: Can I open a bank account without a SIN?

faqItems.answer: A chequing account is for daily use — spending, receiving income, paying bills. A savings account earns more interest but is not designed for frequent transactions. You will likely need both.
faqItems.question: What is the difference between a chequing and savings account?

faqItems.answer: Yes, but bank wire transfer fees can be high ($15–$40 per transfer). Apps like Wise (formerly TransferWise) or Remitly typically offer better exchange rates and lower fees for international transfers.
faqItems.question: Can I send money internationally from my Canadian account?

Bank programs and offers change frequently — always confirm current terms directly with the bank.*

answerSummary: Canada's public healthcare is administered by each province, not the federal government. Most provinces require new residents to wait up to 3 months before provincial health coverage begins. During this period, you should have private travel or health insurance. Apply for your provincial health card as soon as you arrive — do not wait.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Unlike countries with a single national health insurance system, Canada's healthcare is managed by each of its 13 provinces and territories. This means the rules, coverage, and waiting periods are different depending on where you live.

What is consistent across all provinces: once you have coverage, you pay nothing (or very little) for most doctor visits, hospital stays, and medically necessary procedures. However, many services are
Prescription medications (unless through a separate provincial drug benefit)
Most provinces have a waiting period before your provincial health card activates. During this time, you are responsible for all healthcare costs out of pocket — unless you have private insurance.
are the most newcomer-friendly provinces for immediate healthcare coverage.
How to Apply for Your Provincial Health Card
Apply immediately upon arriving — even if there is a waiting period, the clock starts ticking from when you register, not from when you apply.

Apply at a ServiceOntario location. Bring: passport, immigration document (PR card, work permit, etc.), and proof of Ontario residency (e.g., lease agreement). Website: ontario.ca/page/apply-ohip-and-get-health-card
Apply online through Health Insurance BC or at a Service BC office. Website: hibc.gov.bc.ca
Apply at an Alberta Registry Agent or online. Website: alberta.ca/ahcip
Apply at a RAMQ service point. Note: Quebec has a separate immigration system and its own health rules. Website: ramq.gouv.qc.ca
For other provinces, search "[province name] health card application newcomer" for current instructions.
What to Do During the Waiting Period
If your province has a 3-month waiting period,
Many employers include health benefits in their compensation package that activate immediately. Ask your HR department whether your plan covers you before OHIP/provincial coverage begins.

Several insurers offer short-term health insurance specifically for the waiting period. Plans typically cost $100–$200/month for a single adult and cover emergency hospitalization, doctor visits, and prescription drugs.
If you had travel insurance when entering Canada, extending it through the waiting period is often possible and cost-effective.
What Is Covered After Your Card Activates?
Coverage varies by province, but most plans cover:
Hospital stays and emergency room visits
Surgery and diagnostic tests (X-rays, MRIs, blood tests)
Mental health visits (with a doctor referral)
Prescription drugs (separate provincial drug plans may apply — check your province)
For prescription drug coverage, look into your employer's benefits plan, or check whether your province has a low-income drug benefit program.

Having a health card does not automatically give you a family doctor (general practitioner or GP). Canada currently has a shortage of family doctors in many areas, and some patients wait 1–2+ years to be assigned one.
Steps to find a family doctor:
Check your province's official "accepting new patients" registry
Visit a walk-in clinic for non-emergency care while you wait
Register with Health Care Connect (Ontario) or your provincial equivalent
Ask your employer or community group for recommendations

exampleScenarios.body: Carlos moved from Brazil to Toronto as a permanent resident in July. He knew Ontario had a 3-month OHIP waiting period, so before leaving Brazil he purchased a 90-day temporary health insurance plan from Blue Cross for $175/month. On his first week in Toronto, he applied at a ServiceOntario office. Three months later, his OHIP card arrived in the mail and he cancelled his temporary insurance plan.

faqItems.answer: Yes. Under the Reciprocal Billing Agreement, most provinces cover emergency and medically necessary care for Canadian residents visiting another province. However, Quebec operates outside this agreement in some cases — check before you travel.
faqItems.question: Does my health card cover me in other provinces?

faqItems.answer: Children (under 18) are covered immediately in some provinces like Ontario — OHIP covers children right away even though adults have a 3-month wait. Check your province's rules specifically for minors.
faqItems.question: Do children have the same waiting period?

faqItems.answer: Contact your provincial health authority. In Ontario, you can replace an OHIP card online through ServiceOntario or in person at a ServiceOntario location.
faqItems.question: I lost my health card. How do I get a replacement?

faqItems.answer: Basic dental care is not covered by provincial health plans. As of 2024, Canada launched the Canadian Dental Care Plan (CDCP) for low- and middle-income households. Check canada.ca/dental for eligibility. *This article is for educational purposes only. Healthcare rules change — always verify current information with your provincial health authority.*

answerSummary: Renting in Canada is governed by provincial law, meaning your rights and obligations depend on which province you live in. Across all provinces, landlords cannot legally discriminate against you based on national or ethnic origin, and tenants have strong legal protections around eviction, rent increases, and maintenance. Understanding the basics before you sign a lease protects you from costly mistakes.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Before You Start Looking: What to Prepare

Finding a rental as a newcomer is competitive. Landlords receive many applications and typically choose the one that presents the least perceived financial risk. Being organised before you start looking significantly improves your chances.
Employment offer letter or current pay stubs on company letterhead
Proof of savings — 3 months of bank statements showing you have funds to cover rent
Previous landlord reference letter (international references are accepted — include email/phone for the landlord)
SIN (not always required, but some landlords request for a credit check)

If you have no Canadian credit history, prepare a short cover letter explaining that you recently arrived, that you have stable employment, and proactively offering first and last month's rent. Many landlords respond well to transparency.

The most common rental type in urban areas. Range from basement suites to high-rise towers. Some condos are rented by individual investors rather than property management companies — the rules and responsiveness vary.
Common in suburban areas. Often more affordable. Typically rented directly by the homeowner. Less formal process but the same legal protections apply.
Multi-level units with some outdoor space. Common in suburbs. Prices sit between apartments and detached homes.
Renting a room in a shared house. Common for single newcomers or students. Lower cost. Not always covered by residential tenancy law (co-tenancy arrangements vary by province).
A lease (also called a tenancy agreement) is a legal contract between you (the tenant) and your landlord. Read it carefully before signing — once signed, both parties are bound by it.
A set period, typically 12 months. You commit to staying (or paying rent) for the full term.
Continues indefinitely until one party provides notice. More flexible but may cost more.
Rent amount and what is included:
Confirm exactly what your monthly rent covers. Common variations:
Utilities included (heat, hydro/electricity, water) — ask which ones
Your province limits how often and by how much rent can be raised. The lease should not promise no increases — ask about what rate applies in your province.
Standard notice periods in most provinces are 60 days (tenant) and 90 days+ (landlord for certain terminations). Review the notice clause.
If you may need to leave before the lease ends, check whether subletting is permitted and under what conditions.
Ontario, BC, and several other provinces legally require landlords to use a standardized lease form. In Ontario, the
(Form LTB) is mandatory for most residential tenancies. If a landlord refuses to provide the standard lease, Ontario tenants have the right to withhold the second month's rent until it is provided.
Always get a written receipt for any money paid to a landlord. Pay by e-transfer or cheque (never cash) so you have a record.

In Ontario, the "last month's rent" deposit is held by the landlord and applied to your final month when you move out. It also earns a small amount of interest (the rate set by the province annually) that must be credited to you.
Landlords cannot enter your unit without proper notice (typically 24 hours written notice in most provinces, except emergencies). They cannot repeatedly enter without reason or harass you to vacate.
Under the Canadian Human Rights Act and provincial human rights codes, landlords cannot refuse to rent to you based on:
decline you based on insufficient income or lack of credit history — but not based on who you are.
The Right to Maintenance and Repairs

Landlords are legally obligated to keep rental units in a good state of repair and compliant with health and safety standards — regardless of what the lease says. A clause in a lease that says "tenant is responsible for all repairs" is not enforceable in most provinces.
(email is fine) to create a record.
Landlords cannot evict you without following the legal process. Valid grounds for eviction generally include:
Causing serious damage to the property
Landlord's own use of the unit (with restrictions)

Evictions require formal written notice and, if disputed, a hearing at the provincial tribunal before any eviction can proceed. A landlord cannot change your locks, remove your belongings, or shut off utilities to force you out — this is illegal in all provinces.
Rent Control: What You Need to Know
Rent increases are limited to the annual
set by the province (2.5% for 2025). However, this applies only to units
. Units built after that date are exempt from rent control and can be increased by any amount with proper notice.
Annual increases are capped at the inflation rate (CPI), with a maximum of 3% for 2025. Applies to all rental units.
No provincial rent control. Landlords can increase rent once every 365 days with 3 months' written notice — with no cap on the amount.
Rent increases must be "justified" and can be contested at the Tribunal administratif du logement (TAL). There is no fixed maximum percentage, but the TAL has guidelines for reasonable increases.
Move-In Inspection: Do Not Skip This Step
Before moving in (or on move-in day), complete a
with your landlord. Document the condition of every room, appliance, and fixture. Take dated photographs.
Why this matters: When you move out, the landlord can deduct from your deposit (in provinces with damage deposits) only for damage
that occurred during your tenancy. Without a move-in inspection record, they can claim pre-existing damage was caused by you.
In BC, a move-in inspection is legally required. In Ontario, it is strongly recommended even though not mandatory.
How to Handle Disputes with Your Landlord
If a dispute arises (unreturned deposit, failure to repair, illegal rent increase, unlawful eviction attempt), every province has a free or low-cost tribunal:
Filing a complaint is free or low-cost. Bring your lease, communication records, photos, and receipts.

exampleScenarios.body: Oluwaseun signed a 12-month lease on a 1-bedroom apartment in Hamilton, Ontario. At move-in, he and his landlord completed a written inspection together and photographed a pre-existing scratch on the hardwood floor and a stain on the bathroom ceiling. After 12 months, he gave proper 60-day notice and moved out. The landlord tried to deduct $400 from his last month's rent for the hardwood scratch. Oluwaseun provided the signed move-in inspection report and photos to the Landlord and Tenant Board. The LTB ruled in his favour — the pre-existing damage was documented.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: In Ontario, landlords cannot require post-dated cheques. Tenants have the right to pay by a method of their choosing (cash, cheque, e-transfer). In other provinces, rules vary — but requiring 12 months of post-dated cheques upfront is uncommon and likely unenforceable.
faqItems.question: My landlord asked for a full year of post-dated cheques. Is that legal?

faqItems.answer: Generally no — rent cannot be increased during a fixed-term lease period. Increases typically take effect only after the lease converts to month-to-month, or upon renewal.
faqItems.question: Can my landlord increase my rent during a fixed-term lease?

faqItems.answer: Document the problem in writing (email your landlord). If they do not act within a reasonable time, file a complaint with the provincial Landlord and Tenant Tribunal. Pest control is a landlord maintenance obligation in all provinces.
faqItems.question: My building has mice and my landlord won't fix it. What can I do?

faqItems.answer: In most provinces, refusing to rent to families with children is illegal discrimination under provincial human rights codes. "No children" clauses in leases are generally unenforceable (except in designated seniors-only buildings that meet specific criteria). *Sources: CMHC — Finding a Place to Rent (cmhc-schl.gc.ca); Ontario Residential Tenancies Act; BC Residential Tenancy Act; Alberta Residential Tenancies Act; Canadian Human Rights Act. This article is for educational purposes and does not constitute legal advice.*
faqItems.question: Can I bring my children? My landlord said "no children allowed."?

seoDescription: Everything newcomers need to know about renting in Canada — lease types, tenant rights by province, what landlords can ask, damage deposits, and how to handle disputes.
seoTitle: Renting in Canada: Complete Guide for Newcomers | Maple Insight
summary: From signing your first lease to understanding your rights as a tenant, this guide walks you through every step of renting in Canada — including what landlords can and cannot ask of you.
title: Renting in Canada: A Complete Newcomer's Guide

answerSummary: For most newcomers, renting makes more financial sense in the first 1–3 years in Canada, even if buying is the long-term goal. You need a credit history, a stable income, and a down payment before buying becomes viable. Renting gives you time to build all three while you decide which city and neighbourhood are right for you.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: The Canadian Housing Market: Context for Newcomers
Canada has some of the most expensive housing in the world, particularly in Toronto and Vancouver. Average home prices in major cities range from $700,000 to $1.2 million+.
To buy a home in Canada, you will generally need:
for homes under $500,000 (subject to mortgage insurance)
within lender limits (mortgage + debt payments should be under 44% of gross income)
For most newcomers in their first year or two, one or more of these conditions are not yet met — which means renting is both the default and often the smarter choice in the short term.
The Real Cost of Owning vs Renting
The mistake many people make is comparing a monthly mortgage payment to a monthly rent payment and assuming they are equivalent. They are not.
Property taxes (typically 0.5–1.2% of assessed value per year)
Condo maintenance fees (if applicable: $300–$900+/month for condos)
Maintenance and repairs (budget 1–2% of home value per year)
Mortgage default insurance (if down payment is under 20%)
Closing costs (lawyer fees, title insurance, home inspection: ~$2,000–$5,000)

The flexibility of renting — the ability to move cities, change neighbourhoods, or upsize/downsize without the transaction costs of selling — has significant value that the mortgage-vs-rent comparison often ignores.
You are not yet sure which city or neighbourhood you want to settle in long-term
You have been in Canada less than 2 years and do not yet have a strong credit history
You do not have a stable 2-year employment history in Canada
You have not yet accumulated a sufficient down payment
You plan to move within 3–5 years (transaction costs of buying and selling make short-term homeownership expensive)
You are in a market where rent is much cheaper relative to buying costs (price-to-rent ratio is high)
You have stable income, a 2+ year credit history, and a minimum down payment
You plan to stay in the same city for at least 5 years
You want to build equity over time (principal paydown + appreciation)
You have a growing family and want stability in your housing
Mortgage payments are comparable to or only slightly more than rent in your market
You can afford the full carrying costs (not just the mortgage)
Renting a 2-bedroom in a Toronto suburb:

The difference of $2,330/month invested instead would generate significant wealth over time. However, the homeowner is also building equity (~$1,000/month in principal paydown in Year 1) and may benefit from appreciation. The break-even point depends heavily on home price appreciation assumptions.
Use our [Rent vs Buy Calculator](/tools/mortgage-comparison) to model your specific scenario.

exampleScenarios.body: Aisha arrived in Ottawa in 2024 and was immediately tempted to buy. She had $80,000 in savings (enough for a down payment), but no Canadian credit history and only 8 months of employment at a new company. She chose to rent a $1,900/month 2-bedroom for 2 years, invested the difference in her TFSA and FHSA, and built both her credit score and her employment track record. By 2026, she had a 720+ credit score, 2 years of T4 slips, and an additional $30,000 saved (including FHSA contributions) — putting her in a significantly stronger buying position.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Some lenders have newcomer mortgage programs that require less employment history — typically 3–12 months of Canadian employment if you have a strong international credit history and a larger down payment. Ask your bank about newcomer mortgage programs.

faqItems.answer: The Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) requires homebuyers with less than 20% down payment to purchase mortgage insurance, which protects the lender if you default. author: Maple Insight

Any growth inside the RESP is not taxed while it sits in the account. When withdrawn for education, it is taxed in the student's hands — typically at a very low rate because students usually have little income.
This is the core reason the RESP is so compelling. The federal government automatically deposits a

answerSummary: Every Canadian resident is entitled to a free copy of their credit report from both Equifax and TransUnion once per year. Many banks and free apps also show your score in real time. Checking your own score is a "soft inquiry" — it does not affect your score at all. You should review your report at least once per year to catch errors, which are more common than most people realize.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Why Reviewing Your Credit Report Matters
Your credit report is the data behind your credit score. It contains:
A list of every credit account you have (or have had) in Canada
Hard inquiries (when a lender checked your credit)
Any collections, bankruptcies, or negative marks

Errors on credit reports are surprisingly common — and they can hurt your score significantly. One study estimated that a meaningful percentage of Canadians have at least one error on their credit report. As a newcomer, you have an additional risk: identity fraud, where someone opens credit in your name using your SIN.
Reviewing your report annually catches errors before they cause real harm.
Where to Get Your Free Credit Report
From the Credit Bureaus Directly (Official, Free)
Free annual credit report: Available online or by mail
Free annual credit report: Available online or by mail
When you request your report from the bureau directly, you are getting the full official report — every account, every inquiry, every item on file.
These services show your credit score for free, updated regularly:
These services are free because they show you targeted product offers (credit cards, loans) — you are never obligated to use any of them.
How to Read Your Credit Report
Your credit report is divided into several sections:
Your name, current and previous addresses, date of birth, SIN (partially masked), and employment information.
Ensure your name and SIN are correct. Incorrect information here can indicate identity fraud or a mix-up with another person's file.
A list of all credit accounts: credit cards, lines of credit, car loans, mortgages, student loans.
For each account, you will see:
Credit limit or original loan amount
Payment history (typically shown as months of on-time vs. late payments)
Are there accounts you do not recognize? (Possible fraud — act immediately)
Are any accounts incorrectly marked as late when you paid on time?
Are closed accounts still showing a balance?
A list of every time a lender or organization requested your credit file.
When you apply for credit (credit card, loan, etc.). These appear for 3 years and can slightly reduce your score temporarily.
When you check your own score, or when a lender does a background check. These are only visible to you, not to other lenders, and do not affect your score.
Are there hard inquiries you do not recognize? An unknown inquiry can indicate someone applied for credit in your name.
Bankruptcies, consumer proposals, judgments. These have serious negative impacts on credit.
Common Errors and How to Dispute Them
Accounts that are not yours (fraud or a mix-up with someone with a similar name/SIN)
Old negative information that should have been removed (most negative items stay for 6–7 years)
Gather your evidence (bank statements, payment confirmations, correspondence with the lender)
Contact the credit bureau in writing
Equifax: Use the online dispute form at equifax.ca, or mail a written request with your evidence to their dispute department
TransUnion: Use the online dispute portal at transunion.ca

Contact the lender directly The credit bureau will investigate by contacting the lender who reported the information. You can speed this up by also contacting the lender directly to correct the error at the source.
Follow up in 30–60 days Credit bureaus are required to investigate disputes within 30 days. If the investigation confirms an error, it must be corrected.

exampleScenarios.body:
Amara had been in Canada for 18 months and was getting ready to apply for a car loan. Before applying, she checked her Equifax report through Borrowell. She noticed an account for a credit card she had never applied for — it was $320 in arrears. This was identity fraud. She immediately:
1. Called Equifax's fraud line to flag the account
2. Filed a fraud alert on her file (which notifies lenders to take extra steps to verify identity)
3. Reported the fraud to the CRA and the police
4. Contacted the bank that issued the fraudulent card
The fraudulent account was removed within 45 days. Without checking her report, she would have discovered the problem only when her car loan was declined.
